Boettgerilla pallens, common name the worm slug, is a European species of air-breathing land slug, a terrestrial pulmonate gastropod mollusk in the family Boettgerillidae.
Callophrys xami, commonly referred to as the xami hairstreak or green hairstreak, is a butterfly included in the subgenus Xamia and the genus Callophrys in the family Lycaenidae.
The Xantus’ swimming crab is a marine crustacean characterized by flattened, paddle-like hind legs used for swimming and burying in sandy or muddy habitats.
Mimoides xeniades is a butterfly in the family Papilionidae.
Gymnangium montagui is a species of cnidarian in the family, Aglaopheniidae, first described in 1912 by Armand Billard as Halicornaria montagui, from a specimen found off Roskoff.
Scathophaga stercoraria, commonly known as the yellow dung fly  or the golden dung fly, is one of the most familiar and abundant flies in many parts of the Northern Hemisphere.
Eurytides marcellus, the zebra swallowtail, is a swallowtail butterfly native to the eastern United States and south-eastern Canada.

notes: The capybara is the world's largest living rodent, native to South America, where it inhabits savannas and dense forests near lakes, rivers, and wetlands.

diet: Preys on lemurs, birds, and small mammals
notes: The fossa is the largest carnivorous mammal endemic to Madagascar and the island's apex predator, capable of preying on lemurs almost as large as itself.

diet: Feeds on ants, termites, and earthworms
notes: The short-beaked echidna is one of four living species of echidna and one of only five living monotremes — egg-laying mammals — found across Australia and New Guinea.

diet: Preys on sloths, monkeys, and large lizards
notes: The harpy eagle is one of the world's largest and most powerful eagles, inhabiting the tropical lowland rainforests of Central and South America.

diet: Preys on small mammals, particularly long-haired rats
notes: The inland taipan, also known as the fierce snake, is endemic to Australia and holds the record for the most toxic venom of any snake in the world.

diet: Eats crabs, shrimp, and small fish
notes: The greater blue-ringed octopus is one of the world's most venomous marine animals, found in tidal pools and coral reefs across the Pacific and Indian Oceans.

diet: Eats small fish, crabs, and worms
notes: The mimic octopus is a unique species that can impersonate other marine animals such as flatfish, lionfish, and sea snakes to deter predators, and is native to the tropical Indo-Pacific.

diet: Preys on insects, lizards, and small vertebrates
notes: The Philippine tarsier is one of the smallest primates in the world, notable for its enormous eyes — each as large as its entire brain — and its inability to rotate its eyeballs.
The Sunda pangolin is a critically endangered species covered in large, protective keratin scales, native to Southeast Asia, and is the world's most trafficked wild mammal.
The goblin shark is a rare, deep-sea shark species with a distinctive elongated, flattened snout and protrusible jaws, often described as a living fossil.

diet: Eats mysid shrimp and small crustaceans
notes: The leafy sea dragon is a marine fish closely related to seahorses, native to the southern coast of Australia, and is covered with leaf-like protrusions used for camouflage.

notes: The vampire squid is a small deep-sea cephalopod that is neither a true squid nor an octopus, inhabiting the oxygen minimum zones of tropical and subtropical oceans at depths of 600–900 metres.

diet: Eats fallen fruit, fungi, small vertebrates, and insects
notes: The southern cassowary is a large flightless bird native to the tropical rainforests of New Guinea and northeastern Australia, considered the world's most dangerous bird due to its powerful kick and dagger-like claws.

diet: Eats insects, spiders, small lizards, and rodents
notes: The frilled-neck lizard, also known as the frilled lizard, is native to northern Australia and southern New Guinea and is famous for the large frill of skin around its neck which it spreads when threatened.

diet: Preys on fish, crayfish, frogs, and insects
notes: The Japanese giant salamander is the second-largest salamander in the world, reaching up to 1.5 metres, and is endemic to the mountain streams of western Japan.

notes: The geoduck is the largest burrowing clam in the world, native to the Pacific Northwest coast of North America, and can live more than 150 years.
